elements.sent-2411-001: A class of linear parabolic equations is considered.
elements.sent-2411-002: We derive a framework for the a posteriori error analysis of time discretisations by Richardson extrapolation of arbitrary order combined with finite element discretisations in space.
elements.sent-2411-003: We use the idea of elliptic reconstructions and certain bounds for the Green’s function of the parabolic operator.
elements.sent-2411-004: The crucial point in the analysis is the design of suitable polynomial reconstructions in time from approximations that are given only in the mesh points.
elements.sent-2411-005: Keywords: parabolic problems, maximum-norm a posteriori error estimates, backward Euler, extrapolation, elliptic reconstructions, Green's function.
elements.sent-2411-007: Given a second-order linear elliptic operator $\m{L}$ in an open spatial domain $\Omega\subset\RR^n$ with Lipschitz boundary, we consider the linear parabolic equation \begin{subequations \begin{alignat {2 \m{K u \coloneqq \pt_t u + \m{L u = f\,, \quad \text{in \quad Q\coloneqq\Omega \times (0,T], \intertext{subject to the initial condition u(x,0) = u^0(x)\,, \text{for \quad x\in\bar{\Omega , \intertext{and the homogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition u(x,t) = 0\,, \text{for \quad (x,t)\in \pt\Omega \times [0,T].}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: One classical example is Thom\'ee's monograph \cite{MR2249024 which gives a comprehensive overview of the FEM for~\eqref{problem and related equations.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The subject is also elaborated on in various textbooks, e.g.~\cite{MR2362757, MR3015004 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The majority of these publications focus on a priori error estimation, i.e. convergence results given in terms of the mesh size and the regularity of the exact solution.
elements.sent-2411-011: More recently, the derivation of a posteriori error bounds has attracted the attention of many researchers.
elements.sent-2411-012: This approach yields error bounds in terms of the computed numerical solution, and therefore yields computable bounds on the error.
elements.sent-2411-013: Most publications to date study the error in $L_2$-norms or in energy norms naturally induced by the variational formulation of the problem, see e.g.~\cite{MR695602,MR695603,MR1043607 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In contrast, our focus is on a posteriori error bounds in the maximum norm.
elements.sent-2411-015: The first such results were probably given in~\cite{MR1335652 , however the proofs were deferred to a later paper which seems to have never been published.}
elements.sent-2411-016: A key publication is~\cite{MR2034895 by Makridakis and Nochetto who introduced the concept of elliptic reconstructions.}
elements.sent-2411-017: This idea was used in a number of publications to study the FEM combined with various time discretisations: backward Euler, Crank Nicolson and discontinuous Galerkin~\cite{MR2519598, MR2629992, MR3056758 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The techniques in those papers are all tailored to the particular method(s) under consideration and therefore differ to some extend.
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, different --- although related --- stability results have been used.
elements.sent-2411-020: In~\cite{MR4755064 a common framework for the maximum-norm a posteriori error analysis of second order time discretisations was derived and applied to various time discretisations: backward Euler, Crank-Nicolson, a two-step extrapolation method based on backward Euler, the BDF-2 method, the Lobatto IIIC method and a two-stage SDIRK method.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In the present paper we shall study the use of Richardson extrapolations of arbitrary order $L\ge2$ for the time discretisation, while in space any finite-element method might be used.
elements.sent-2411-023: There the point-wise approximations in the nodes of the temporal mesh were joined by ``straight lines'' (i.e. $\Pi_1$-interpolation) to obtain a continuous approximation on the whole time interval $[0,T]$.
elements.sent-2411-024: For $L>2$ the design of this temporal reconstruction is more delicate.
elements.sent-2411-025: To match to order $L$ of the method, on each subinterval the reconstruction should use a polynomial of degree $L-1$.
elements.sent-2411-026: However, only in the mesh points the approximation is of order $L$, all other auxilliary approximations constructed during the extrapolation process are of lower order and therefore useless for our purpose.
elements.sent-2411-027: This crucial issue will be addressed in~\autoref{sssect:apost-para-temp_rec .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-029: In \autoref{sect:weak we specify our assumptions on the data of problem~\eqref{problem and recapitulate certain aspects of the existence theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: In \autoref{sect:ana we develop our a posteriori error analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-031: We formulate the assumptions for the analysis in \autoref{ssect:apost-ell (existence of error estimators for the elliptic problems) and in \autoref{ssect:green (bounds on the Green's function of the parabolic problem).}}
elements.sent-2411-032: In \autoref{ssect:reco the concept of elliptic reconstructions is recalled, while the main result is derived in \autoref{ssect:apost-para .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: We shall study~\eqref{problem in its standard variational form, cf.~\cite[\S5.1.1]{MR2362757 .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The appropriate Gelfand triple consists of the spaces \begin{equation}V = H_0^1(\Omega), \quad H= L_2(\Omega) \quadand\quad V^* = H^{-1}(\Omega)\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: Moreover, by $a(\cdot,\cdot) \colon V \times V \to \RR$ we denote the bilinear form associated with the elliptic operator $\m{L}$, while $\dual{\cdot {\cdot}\colon V^*\times V \to \RR$ is the duality pairing and $\scal{\cdot {\cdot}\colon H\times H\to\RR$ is the scalar product in~$H$.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Also we shall denote by $\norm{\cdot _{q,D}$ the standard norm in $L_q(D)$, $q\in[1,\infty]$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The solution $u$ of~\eqref{problem may be considered as a mapping $[0,T] \to V\colon t \mapsto u(t)$, and we will denote its (temporal) derivative by $u'$ (and $\pt_t u$).. Let \begin{equation}W_2^1(0,T;V,H) \coloneqq \left\{ v \in L_2(0,T;V) \colon v'\in L_2(0,T;V^*)\right\}\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Then our variational formulation of~\eqref{problem reads: Given $u^0\in H$ and $F\in L_2(0,T;V^*)$, find $u\in W_2^1(0,T;V,H)$ such that \begin{subequations \begin{alignat {2 \frac{\D {\D t \bigscal{u(t) {\chi + a\bigscal{u(t) {\chi = \bigdual{F(t) {\chi \quad \forall \chi\in V, \ \ t\in(0,T], \intertext{and u(0)=u^0.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In the sequell we shall assume that the source term $F$ is more regular and can be represented as \mbox{$\dual{F(t) {\chi =\scal{f {\chi}$}, $\forall v\in V$, with a function $f\in L_2(0,T;H)$ We are interested in maximum-norm error estimates.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Therefore, we have to make further assumptions on the data to ensure that the solution can be evaluated pointwise.
elements.sent-2411-041: To this end, we assume that the intial and boundary data satisfy the zero-th order compatibility condition, i.e. $u^0 = 0$ on $\pt\Omega$, and that $u^0$ is Hölder continuous in $\bar\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Under standard assumptions on $f$ and $\m{L}$, problem~\eqref{problem possesses a unique solution that is continuous on $\bar{Q}$; see~\cite[\S5, Theorem 6.4]{0174.15403 .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Let a mesh in time be given by \begin{equation}\omega \colon 0=t_0<t_1<\ldots<t_M=T,\end{equation} with mesh intervals \mbox{$I_j\coloneqq(t_{j-1 ,t_j)$} and step sizes \mbox{$\tau_j\coloneqq t_j-t_{j-1}$}, $j=1,2,\dots,M$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Furthermore, for $\xi\in[0,1]$, set \mbox{$t_{j-\xi}\coloneqq t_j-\xi\tau_j$}, and for any function $v\in C^0\left([0,T],L_2(\Omega)\right)$ let \mbox{$v^{j-\xi \coloneqq v(t_{j-\xi )$}, $j=0,1,\dots,M$.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: For the temporal discretisation of~\eqref{weak we shall use Richardson extrapolation with $L\ge2$ extrapolation steps applied to the backward Euler method.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We slightly modify~\eqref{weak by replacing $f$ by $\hat{f}$, where $\hat{f}\vert_{I_j}\in \PP_{L-1}$, $j=1,\dots,M$, interpolates $f$ at the mesh points and on $L-2$ further points on each mesh interval $I_j$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: For the spatial discretisation, let $V_h$ be a finite dimentional (FE-)subspace of $V$, and let $a_h\scal{\cdot {\cdot}$ and $\scal{\cdot {\cdot _h$ be approximations of the bilinear form $a\scal{\cdot {\cdot}$ and of the scalar product $\scal{\cdot {\cdot}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: These may involve quadrature, for example.
elements.sent-2411-049: Furthermore, let $u_h^0\in V_h$ be an approximation of the initial condition $u^0$, Then our discretisation is as follows: \begin{alg*}\mbox{ S1: Initialise constants [1.5ex] \qquad $\displaystyle \alpha_\ell \coloneqq \frac{\abs{\bs{\mathrm{e ^\ell \vert \bs{B {\abs{\bs{1 ^{\phantom{\ell \vert \bs{B }\,, \ \ \text{where \ \ \bs{B}\coloneqq \left(b_{ik}\right) \in \RR^{L,L-1}\,, \ \ b_{im =1/i^m.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Our analysis uses three main ingredients: \begin{itemize* \item a posteriori error bounds for the elliptic problem $\m{L y=g$, see \autoref{ssect:apost-ell , \item bounds for the Green's function associated with the parabolic operator $\m{K}$, see \autoref{ssect:green and \item the idea of elliptic reconstructions introduced by Makridakis and Nochetto~\cite{MR2034895 , see \autoref{ssect:reco .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: As mentioned earlier, the crucial point will be the design of the temporal reconstruction of $u_h$.
elements.subsection-2411-001: A posteriori error estimation for the elliptic problem
elements.sent-2411-052: Given $g\in H$, consider the elliptic boundary-value problem of finding $y\in V$ such that \begin{equation}\label{prob:ell} a\scal{y}{\chi} = \scal{g}{\chi}\,, \ \ \forall \chi\in V,\end{equation} and its discretisation of finding $y_h\in V_h$ such that \begin{equation}\label{FEM:ell} a_h\scal{y_h}{\chi} = \scal{g}{\chi}_h\,, \ \ \forall \chi\in V_h.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-054: They give a posteriori error bounds for arbitrary order FEM on quasiuniform triangulations.
elements.sent-2411-055: A posteriori error estimates are established that are robust in the perturbation parameter.
elements.sent-2411-056: Furthermore, in~\cite{MR3419888 for the same problem $P_1$-FEM on anisotropic meshes are investigated, while Kopteva and Rankin~\cite{MR4625899 derive related results for the discontinuous Galerkin FEM.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: A posteriori error bounds in the maximum norm are given for classical conforming and for stabilised FEM.
elements.sent-2411-058: In contrast to the afore mentioned contributions all constants appearing in the error estimator are given explicitly.. \end{itemize*.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Let the Green's function associated with $\m{K}$ and an arbitrary point $x\in\Omega$ be denoted by $\m{G}$, Then for all $\phi\in W^1_2\left(0,T;V,H\right)$ \begin{equation}\label{green-rep} \phi(x,t) = \bigscal{\phi(0)}{\m{G}(t)} + \int_0^t \bigdual{\bigl(\m{K}\phi\bigr)(s)}{\m{G}(t-s)} \D s, \ \ \text{see~\cite{0144.34903}.}\end{equation} The Green's function \mbox{$\m{G \colon \bar{\Omega}\times[0,T]\to\RR$}, $t \in (0,T]$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Given approximations $u_h\in C^0\bigl([0,T],V_h\bigr)$ of $u$ and $\hat{f}\in C^0\bigl([0,T],L_2(\Omega)\bigr)$ of $f$, we define $\psi\in C^0\bigl([0,T],V_h\bigr)$ by \begin{equation}\label{reconstr-psi} \scal{\psi(t)}{\chi}_h = a_h\scal{u_h(t)}{\chi} - \scal{\hat{f}(t)}{\chi}_h \quad \forall \ \chi\in V_h\,, \ \ t\in[0,T].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: This can be reformulated as an ``elliptic'' problem: \begin{equation}\label{elliptic:discr} a_h\scal{u_h(t)}{\chi} = \scal{\hat{f} + \psi(t)}{\chi}_h \quad \forall \ \chi\in V_h, \ \ t\in[0,T].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: Next, let $R(t)\in C^0\bigl([0,T],H_0^1(\Omega)\bigr)$ solve \begin{equation}\label{reconstr} a\scal{R(t)}{\chi} = \scal{\hat{f}(t)+\psi(t)}{\chi} \quad \forall \ \chi\in H_0^1(\Omega)\,, \ \ t\in[0,T],\end{equation} or for short: \begin{equation}\label{reco-LRfpsi} \m{L}R(t) = \hat{f}(t)+\psi(t)\,, \ \ t\in[0,T].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: The function $R$ is referred to as the elliptic reconstruction of $u_h$, \cite{MR2034895 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Now, $u_h(t)$ can be regarded as the finite-element approximation of $R(t)$ obtained by~\eqref{elliptic:discr .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Its error $E(t)\coloneqq R(t)-u_h(t)$, $t\in[0,T]$, can be bounded using the estimator from~\autoref{ssect:apost-ell : \begin{equation}\label{reconstr-esti} \norm{E(t)}_\infty = \norm{\bigl(u_h - R\bigr)(t)}_\infty \le \eta_ell(t) \coloneqq \eta\left(u_h(t), \bigl(\hat{f}+\psi\bigr)(t)\right)\,, \ \ t\in[0,T].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-066: By~\eqref{reconstr-psi and the inner loop of the time stepping procedure we have for the Richardson extrapolation \begin{equation}\label{psi-comp} \psi(t_j) = \psi^j = - \sum_{\ell=1}^L \alpha_\ell \frac{v_\ell^{j} - v_\ell^{j-1/\ell}}{\tau_j/\ell} \,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M.\end{equation}.}
elements.subsection-2411-004: A posteriori error estimation for the parabolic problem
elements.sent-2411-067: We are now in a position to derive the main result of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-068: We use the Green's function representation~\eqref{green-rep with $\phi$ replaced by $u-u_h$ to express the error at final time $T$ and for any $x\in\Omega$ as \begin{equation}\label{rep-error} \left(u(T)-u_h^M\right)(x) = \bigl(u-u_h\bigr)(x,T) = \bigscal{u^0-u_h^0}{\m{G}(T)} + \int_0^T \bigdual{\bigl(\m{K}(u-u_h\bigr)(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-069: This involves the residual of $u_h$ in the differential equation for which we have the representation \begin{equation}\m{K}(u-u_h)(s) = f(s) - \mL u_h(s) - \pt_t u_h(s) = \bigl(f-\hat{f}\bigr)(s) + \mL E(s) - \psi(t) - \pt_t u_h(s)\,, \ \ s\in [0,T] \setminus \omega_t,\end{equation} by~\eqref{reco-LRfpsi and $E=R-u_h$.}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\m{K}(u-u_h)(s) = f(s) - \mL u_h(s) - \pt_t u_h(s) = \bigl(f-\hat{f}\bigr)(s) + \mL E(s) - \psi(t) - \pt_t u_h(s)\,, \ \ s\in [0,T] \setminus \omega_t,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-070: This is substituted into~\eqref{rep-error to give \begin{equation}\label{error-rep-discr}  \left(u(T)-u_h^M\right)(x) = \scal{u^0-u^0_h}{\m{G}(T)} + \int_{0}^{T}\scal{\bigl(f-\hat{f}\bigr)(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s \qquad + \int_0^T \dual{\mL E(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s - \int_0^T \dual{\left(\psi+\pt_t u_h\right)(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s\,. \end{equation} The first two terms can be bounded using H\"older's inequality and \eqref{source:ass : \begin{equation}\label{est-init} \abs{\scal{u^0-u_h^0}{\m{G}(T)}} \le \kappa_0 \sigma_0 \norm{u^0-u_h^0}_{\infty,\Omega} \intertext{and}\label{est-F} \abs{\int_{I_j} \scal{\bigl(f-\hat{f}\bigr)(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)}\D s} \le \kappa_0 \sigma_j \int_{I_j} \norm{\bigl(f-\hat{f}\bigr)(s)}_{\infty,\Omega} \D s\,,\end{equation} where we have set \begin{equation}\label{def:sigma_j} \sigma_j \coloneqq \exp\left(-\gamma\left(T-t_j\right)\right)\,, \ \ j=0,\dots,M.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The remaining two terms in~\eqref{error-rep-discr , namely \begin{equation}\int_0^T \dual{\mL E(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s \quad and \quad \int_0^T \dual{\left(\psi+\pt_t u_h\right)(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s\end{equation} have to be treated individually depending on the (piecewise) polynomial degree of the temporal reconstruction $u_h$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The temporal reconstruction of the $u_h^j$, $j=0,1,\dots,N$, will be designed in a systematic manner.
elements.sent-2411-073: Starting from a continuous piecewise $\PP_1$-interpolation, on each interval $I_j$ we add polynomial bubbles.
elements.sent-2411-074: A suitable basis for these are the integrated Legendre polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-075: Let $P_i$ denote the Legendre polynomial of order $i\in\NN_0$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Then the integrated Legendre polynomials $N_i$ are defined by \begin{equation}N_{i+1}(\xi) \coloneqq \int_{-1}^\xi P_i(\zeta) \D \zeta = \left(\xi^2-1\right) \frac{P_i'(\xi)}{i(i+1)}\,, \ \ \ i\in\NN.\end{equation}. Note that \begin{equation}N_{i+1} = \frac{P_{i+1}-P_{i-1}}{2i+1}\quad and\quad N_{i+1}(-1) = N_{i+1}(1) = 0\,, \ \ \ i\in\NN.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-077: Next, we define the temporal reconstruction for the $L$-step Richardson extrapolation method, $L\ge2$.
elements.sent-2411-078: To this end, let both the reconstruction $u_h$ and the interpolant $\hat{f}$ of $f$ be of polynomial degree $L-1$ on each~$\bar{I _j$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: This degree matches the order of the time discretisation.
elements.sent-2411-080: Because of~\eqref{reconstr-psi , both the function $\psi$ and the elliptic reconstruction $R$ are of polynomial degree $L-1$ on $\bar{I _j$ too.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Any function $\phi\in C^0\left([0,T],V\right)$ that is of piecewise polynomial degree $L-1$ can be written as \begin{equation}\label{t-poly-p} \phi = \Delta^0 \phi^j + \Delta^1 \phi^j P_1\circ \xi_j + \sum_{i=2}^{L-1} \Delta^i\phi^j N_{i}\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ \bar{I}_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: Note that the $\Delta^i\phi^j$, $i=2,\dots,p$, uniquely define $\phi$ on $\bar{I _j$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: On the other hand, prescribing the values of $\phi$ at additional $L-2$ points in $I_j$ will determine the $\Delta^i\phi^j$.
elements.sent-2411-084: While the $\Delta^i\hat{f ^j$ are uniquely defined by the particular interpolation of $f$ used in the implementation of the Richardson extrapolation procedure, the $\Delta^i u_h^j$, $i=2,\dots,L-1$, have to be chosen carefully to obtain suitable a posteriori error bounds.}
elements.sent-2411-085: As stated, on each $I_j$ the functions/reconstructions $\hat{f}$, $u_h$, $\psi$ and $R$ are elements of $\PP_{L-1}$, and in view of~\eqref{reconstr-psi , \eqref{elliptic:discr and~\eqref{reconstr , we have \begin{equation}\label{reconstr-delta-psi} a_h\scal{\Delta^iu_h^j}{\chi} = \scal{\Delta^i\psi^j + \Delta^i \hat{f}^j}{\chi}_h \quad \forall \ \chi\in H_0^1(\Omega)\,, \ \ i=0,1,\dots,L-1,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}a\scal{\Delta^i R^j}{\chi} = \scal{\Delta^i \hat{f}^j+\Delta^i \psi^j}{\chi} \quad \forall \ \chi\in H_0^1(\Omega)\,, \ \ i=0,1,\dots,L-1.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The latter and~\eqref{reconstr-esti imply \begin{equation}\label{reconstr-esti-delta} \norm{\Delta^i E^j}_\infty \le \eta^j_ell,i \coloneqq \eta\left(\Delta^i u_h^j, \Delta^i \hat{f}^j+\Delta^i\psi^j\right)\,, \quad i=0,1,\dots,L-1, \ \ j=1,\dots,M.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-087: We now define the $\Delta^i u_h^j$ and $\Delta^i \psi^j$, $i=2,\dots,L-1$, by carefully examining $\psi + \pt_t u_h$, the last term in~\eqref{error-rep-discr .}
elements.sent-2411-088: For $L=2$, we have \begin{equation}\label{psi-ptuh-2} \psi+\pt_t u_h = \Delta^0 \psi^j + \frac{2\Delta^1 u_h^j}{\tau_j} + \Delta^1\psi^j P_1\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M,\end{equation} while for $L>2$ \begin{equation} \psi+\pt_t u_h = \Delta^0 \psi^j + \frac{2\Delta^1 u_h^j}{\tau_j} - \frac{\Delta^2 \psi^j}{3} + \sum_{i=1}^{L-3} \left\{ \frac{\Delta^i\psi^j}{2i-1} + \frac{2}{\tau_j} \Delta^{i+1} u_h^j - \frac{\Delta^{i+2} \psi^j}{2i+3} \right\} P_i\circ \xi_j \qquad + \left\{ \frac{\Delta^{L-2}\psi^j}{2L-5} + \frac{2}{\tau_j} \Delta^{L-1} u_h^j \right\} P_{L-2}\circ \xi_j + \frac{\Delta^{L-1}\psi^j}{2L-3} P_{L-1}\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M. \end{equation} This gives rise to defining the $\Delta^i\psi^j$ as follows \begin{equation}\frac{\Delta^2 \psi^j}{3} \coloneqq \Delta^0 \psi^j + \frac{2\Delta^1 u_h^j}{\tau_j} \quadand\quad \frac{\Delta^{i+2} \psi^j}{2i+3} \coloneqq \frac{\Delta^i\psi^j}{2i-1} + \frac{2}{\tau_j} \Delta^{i+1} u_h^j\,, \ \ \ i=1, \dots, L-3, \ j=1,\dots,M.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-025: \begin{equation}\label{psi-ptuh-2} \psi+\pt_t u_h = \Delta^0 \psi^j + \frac{2\Delta^1 u_h^j}{\tau_j} + \Delta^1\psi^j P_1\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M,\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-026: \begin{equation} \psi+\pt_t u_h = \Delta^0 \psi^j + \frac{2\Delta^1 u_h^j}{\tau_j} - \frac{\Delta^2 \psi^j}{3} + \sum_{i=1}^{L-3} \left\{ \frac{\Delta^i\psi^j}{2i-1} + \frac{2}{\tau_j} \Delta^{i+1} u_h^j - \frac{\Delta^{i+2} \psi^j}{2i+3} \right\} P_i\circ \xi_j \qquad + \left\{ \frac{\Delta^{L-2}\psi^j}{2L-5} + \frac{2}{\tau_j} \Delta^{L-1} u_h^j \right\} P_{L-2}\circ \xi_j + \frac{\Delta^{L-1}\psi^j}{2L-3} P_{L-1}\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-089: The $\Delta^i u_h^j$ are then computed according to~\eqref{reconstr-delta-psi .}
elements.sent-2411-090: With these definitions of the quantities $\Delta^i\psi^j$ and $\Delta^iu_h^j$, we have \begin{equation}\label{psi-ptuh-L}  \psi+\pt_t u_h = \left\{ \frac{\Delta^{L-2}\psi^j}{2L-5} + \frac{2}{\tau_j} \Delta^{L-1} u_h^j \right\} P_{L-2}\circ \xi_j + \frac{\Delta^{L-1}\psi^j}{2L-3} P_{L-1}\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M. \end{equation} Both~\eqref{psi-ptuh-2 and~\eqref{psi-ptuh-L take the form \begin{equation} \psi+\pt_t u_h = \alpha_L P_{L-2}\circ \xi_j + \beta_L P_{L-1}\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M, \end{equation} where $\alpha_L$ and $\beta_L$ may vary in space, but are constant in time.}}
elements.formula-2411-028: \begin{equation}\label{psi-ptuh-L}  \psi+\pt_t u_h = \left\{ \frac{\Delta^{L-2}\psi^j}{2L-5} + \frac{2}{\tau_j} \Delta^{L-1} u_h^j \right\} P_{L-2}\circ \xi_j + \frac{\Delta^{L-1}\psi^j}{2L-3} P_{L-1}\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M. \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation} \psi+\pt_t u_h = \alpha_L P_{L-2}\circ \xi_j + \beta_L P_{L-1}\circ \xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j\,, \ \ j=1,\dots,M, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-091: The following lemma will be used to bound those terms.
elements.sent-2411-093: Then \begin{equation}\abs{\int_{I_j} \dual{v}{\m{G}(T-s)}P_i\bigl(\xi_j(s)\bigr) \D s} \le \sigma_j \mu_{j,i} \norm{v}_{\infty,\Omega}\,, \ \ \ i=0,1,\dots, \ \ j=1,\dots,M,\end{equation} where the $\sigma_j$ have been defined in~\eqref{def:sigma_j and \begin{equation}\mu_{j,i} =  \kappa_0 \tau_j\,, for \ \ i=0, [1ex] \displaystyle \min \left\{ \frac{\kappa_0 \tau_j}{2} \norm{P_i}_{1,(-1,1)} \,, \frac{\kappa_1}{\tau_j} \int_{I_j} \frac{\bigl(t_j-t\bigr)\bigl(t-t_{j-1}\bigr)}{T-t} \D t + \kappa_1' \frac{\tau_j^2}{24} \right\} , for \ \ i=1,2,\dots \end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-096: If $i\ge 1$, then integration by parts gives \begin{equation}\int_{I_j} \dual{v}{\m{G}(T-s)}P_i\bigl(\xi_j(s)\bigr) \D s = \frac{\tau_j}{2} \int_{I_j} \dual{v}{\m{G}'(T-s)}N_{i+1}\bigl(\xi_j(s)\bigr) \D s \qquad = \frac{2}{\tau_j} \frac{1}{i(i+1)} \int_{I_j} \dual{v}{\m{G}'(T-t)}P_i'\bigl(\xi_j(t)\bigr) \bigl(t-t_j\bigr)\bigl(t-t_{j-1}\bigr) \D t\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-098: Next, by~\eqref{source:ass \begin{equation}\label{intvGP-2} \abs{\int_{I_j} \dual{v}{\m{G}(T-s)}P_i\bigl(\xi_j(s)\bigr) \D s} \le \norm{v}_{\infty,\Omega} \sigma_j \left( \frac{\kappa_1}{\tau_j} \int_{I_j} \frac{\bigl(t_j-s\bigr)\bigl(s-t_{j-1}\bigr)}{T-s} \D s + \kappa_1' \frac{\tau_j^2}{24} \right)\end{equation} Taking the minimum of~\eqref{intvGP-1 and~\eqref{intvGP-2 , we obtain the desired result.}}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Error contributions by the spatial discretisation
elements.sent-2411-100: One term in~\eqref{error-rep-discr still needs our attention: \begin{equation}\sum_{j=1}^M \int_{I_j} \dual{\mL E(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-101: It captures the error contributions arising from the spatial discretisation.
elements.sent-2411-102: The properties of the Green's function yield \begin{equation}\dual{\mL E(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} = \dual{E(s)}{\mL^* \m{G}(T-s)} = - \dual{E(s)}{\pt_t \m{G}(T-s)}\,,\end{equation} which we integrate by parts to give \begin{equation}\sum_{j=1}^M \int_{I_j} \dual{\mL E(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s = \dual{E^M}{\m{G}(0)} - \dual{E^0}{\m{G}(T)} - \sum_{j=1}^M \int_{I_j} \dual{\pt_t E(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-103: H\"older's inequality and \eqref{source:ass give \begin{equation}\label{est-ell0M} \abs{\dual{E^0}{\m{G}(T)}} + \abs{\dual{E^M}{\m{G}(0)}} \le \kappa_0 \left\{\sigma_0 \eta_ell^0 + \eta_ell^M \right\}\end{equation} Next, note that \begin{equation}\pt_t E = \frac{2}{\tau} \sum_{i=1}^{L-1} \Delta^i E^j P_{i-1}\circ\xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j, \ j=1,\dots,M.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-041: \begin{equation}\pt_t E = \frac{2}{\tau} \sum_{i=1}^{L-1} \Delta^i E^j P_{i-1}\circ\xi_j \ \ \ in \ I_j, \ j=1,\dots,M.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-104: Consequently, using~\eqref{source:ass and~\eqref{reconstr-esti-delta \begin{equation}\abs{\int_{I_j} \dual{\pt_t E(s)}{\m{G}(T-s)} \D s} \le \frac{2}{\tau_j} \sum_{i=1}^{L-1} \norm{\Delta^i E^j}_{\infty,\Omega} \int_{I_j} \phi_0(T-s)\abs{P_{i-1}\circ\xi_j} \D s \le \kappa_0 \sigma_j \sum_{i=1}^{L-1} \eta^j_ell,i \norm{P_{i-1}}_{1,(-1,1)}\,.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: We gather the results of \autoref{sssect:apost-para-gen , \autoref{sssect:apost-para-temp_rec and \autoref{sssect:apost-para-spatial to establish our main result.}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Let $u^j_h\in V_h$, $j=0,1,\dots,M$, be the sequence of approximations to $u(t_j)$ generated by the $L$-step extrapolation method introduced in \autoref{sect:discr .}
elements.sent-2411-108: For example, one can evaluate $\abs{u^0-u_h^0}$ at a fixed number of points in each subdomain of the triangulation of $\Omega$ (excluding interpolation of quadrature points).
elements.sent-2411-109: One possibility is the use of sufficiently accurate quadrature rules, that take into account the polynomial degree of the interpolant $\hat{f}$.
elements.sent-2411-110: Consider the following reaction-diffusion equation \begin{subequations \begin{alignat {2 \pt_t u - u_{xx + (5x+6) u = \E^{-4t + \cos\bigl(\pi(x+t)^2\bigr)\,, \quad \text{in \quad (-1,1) \times (0,1], \intertext{subject to the initial condition u(x,0) = \sin \frac{\pi(1+x) {2}\,, \text{for \quad x\in[-1,1], \intertext{and the Dirichlet boundary condition u(x,t) = 0\,, \text{for \quad (x,t)\in \{-1,1\} \times [0,1].}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The exact solution to this problem is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-112: To compute a reference solution, we use a spectral method in space combined with the dG(2) method in time which is of order $5$.
elements.sent-2411-113: This gives an approximation that is accurate close to machine precision.
elements.sent-2411-114: In our experiments, we use the spatial discretisation by $P_1$-FEM analysed in~\cite{MR2334045 and the a posteriori estimator derived therein.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Because our time discretisations (except for the Euler method) are also of second order, we couple spatial and temporal mesh sizes by $h=\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-117: The maximum norm of the error needs to be approximated.
elements.sent-2411-118: We do so by evaluating the error at seven evenly distributed points in each mesh interval: \begin{equation}\bignorm{u(T)-U^M}_{\infty,\Omega} \approx e_M\coloneqq \max_{i=1,\dots,N} \, \max_{r=0,\dots,7} \abs{\left(u(T)-U^M\right)\left(x_{i-1} + rh/7\right)}\,,\end{equation} where the $x_i$, $i=0,\dots,N$, are the nodes of the uniform spatial mesh, and $h$ its mesh size.
elements.sent-2411-119: Tables~\ref{tab:all (a-f) display the results of our test computations.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The first column in each table contains the number of mesh intervals $M$ (with $h=\tau=1/M$), followed by the errors $e_M$ at final time, the experimental order of convergence $p_M$, the error estimator $\eta^{M,0}$ and finally the efficiency $\chi_M$: \begin{equation}p_M\coloneqq \frac{\ln e_{M/2} - \ln e_M}{\ln 2} \quad and \quad \chi_M \coloneqq \frac{e_M}{\eta^{M,0}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-121: Such dependencies are known from other a posteriori error estimates, see e.g.~\cite{05068766 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: A Matlab/Octave program that reproduces the tables of this paper can be found at GitHub: https://github.com/TorstenLinss/???.. \fi \bibliographystyle{plain \def\cprime{$'$}.}
